claim: The extremely high hapax ratio (70.1%) is an artifact of systematic suffix variation: scribes appended variable word-final glyphs from the set {y,n,l,r,o} as inflectional or abbreviation markers, inflating apparent vocabulary size. Stripping these final glyphs should collapse unique word count toward natural-language hapax ratios (~40-50%).
evidence_for: Word-final glyphs {y,n,l,r,o} dominate final positions, consistent with a small set of inflectional suffixes
Top frequent words (daiin, aiin, chedy, shedy) share roots differing only by initial glyph, suggesting stem+affix morphology
Average word length of 5.03 is consistent with a 3-4 glyph stem plus 1-2 glyph suffix
evidence_against: Natural inflected languages (Latin, Italian) typically show hapax ratios of 40-55%, so 70.1% requires strong additional explanation beyond inflection alone
If suffix-stripping were sufficient, the remaining root forms should cluster into recognizable frequency distributions, which has not been demonstrated
test: Apply suffix-stripping by iteratively removing word-final glyphs that belong to {y,n,l,r,o} (up to 2 characters) from each word token, then recompute type-token ratio and hapax ratio on the stripped corpus. Compare stripped hapax ratio to the baseline 70.1%.
test_metric: Hapax ratio of suffix-stripped corpus vs. original; also compute Zipf exponent of stripped corpus and compare to original 0.8946
test_threshold: If stripped hapax ratio falls to 40-55% and Zipf exponent rises toward 1.0, suffix-variation hypothesis is confirmed. If ratio remains above 60%, suffix variation alone is insufficient.

reasoning: The most parsimonious explanation for an anomalously high hapax ratio is systematic surface variation on a smaller underlying root vocabulary. Confirming this would sharply constrain the cipher type and suggest the true vocabulary is 2,000-3,500 roots rather than 8,261 unique surface forms.
